title: Port declarations
analysis:
This task adds port declaration support across the full pipeline: grammar -> parser -> compiler. Ports are typed interface points on structures (like mechanical mounting points) with direction (in/out/bidi), a trait type reference, and optional body containing params, lets, constraints, direction setting, and frame expressions.

Existing patterns to follow:
- Grammar: follows the same pattern as sub_declaration and other _member choices. String literal keywords like 'param', 'let', 'constraint' already work alongside the identifier regex without explicit `word` declaration.
- Parser: PortDecl follows the same struct pattern as SubDecl/ParamDecl with name, type, span, content_hash fields. MemberDecl gets a new Port variant. Lowering follows the same pattern as lower_sub, lower_param.
- Compiler: CompiledPort follows the pattern of SubComponentDecl. Port members become ValueCells with composite member names (port_name.member_name) for scope resolution. TopologyTemplate gets a ports field.
- Port member access via dot notation requires updating the MemberAccess case in compile_expr_guarded to resolve port-qualified names.

Key concern: Using 'in' as a keyword (for port direction) while 'in' is also used as a unit suffix (80in). This works because unit suffixes use token.immediate() which is a separate token type requiring no whitespace. Standalone 'in' with whitespace will match the keyword in port contexts. The trade-off is that 'in', 'out', 'bidi' become reserved words (can't be used as variable/type names), which is acceptable.

Dependency on task 65 (trait grammar/parser) is satisfied. PortDirection enum already exists in reify-types (added in task 53). The trait registry built in task 66 (trait conformance) is available for port type verification.

CompilationScope currently uses HashMap<String, (ValueCellId, Type, Option<ValueCellId>)>. Port members will be registered with composite keys like 'port_name.member_name'. MemberAccess compilation currently emits 'not yet supported' error (line 772-781); this needs updating to recognize port member access patterns.

prerequisites.description: Add port grammar rules to tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js: (1) port_direction_keyword rule as choice('in','out','bidi'), (2) port_declaration rule with fields: name (identifier), optional direction (port_direction_keyword), type (identifier), optional body (port_body), (3) port_body rule with repeat(choice(param_declaration, let_declaration, constraint_declaration, port_direction_setting, port_frame_setting)), (4) port_direction_setting rule: seq('direction','=',field('value',port_direction_keyword)), (5) port_frame_setting rule: seq('frame','=',field('value',_expression)). Add port_declaration to _member choices. Add [$.port_declaration] to conflicts list. Run `npx tree-sitter generate` in tree-sitter-reify/ to regenerate src/parser.c.

Add PortDecl struct and MemberDecl::Port(PortDecl) variant to crates/reify-syntax/src/lib.rs. PortDecl fields: name (String), direction (Option<reify_types::PortDirection>), type_name (String), members (Vec<MemberDecl>), frame_expr (Option<Expr>), span (SourceSpan), content_hash (ContentHash). Add use reify_types::PortDirection import.

Add stub lower_port method to ts_parser.rs Lowering impl that returns None, and add 'port_declaration' case to lower_member that calls lower_port and maps to MemberDecl::Port. This allows the code to compile and existing tests to pass before port lowering is implemented.

steps.description: Write failing test in crates/reify-syntax/tests/port_tests.rs: parse_minimal_port - parses `structure S { port mount : MechanicalPort }` and asserts: 1 declaration, 1 member of type MemberDecl::Port, name='mount', type_name='MechanicalPort', direction=None, empty members vec, frame_expr=None. Uses same parse_decls helper pattern as trait_tests.rs.

Implement lower_port in ts_parser.rs: extract 'name' field (identifier), extract 'type' field (identifier -> type_name), handle optional 'direction' field (port_direction_keyword -> match text to PortDirection variant), handle optional 'body' field (stub returning empty members/None for now). Return Some(PortDecl {...}). Test parse_minimal_port passes.

Write failing test: parse_port_with_direction - parses `structure S { port shaft : in RotaryPort }` and asserts direction=Some(PortDirection::In), type_name='RotaryPort'. Also write parse_port_direction_out testing `port b : out T` -> direction=Some(Out) and parse_port_direction_bidi testing `port c : bidi T` -> direction=Some(Bidi).

Ensure direction parsing works in lower_port. The 'direction' field in the CST is a port_direction_keyword node. Map its text content: 'in' -> PortDirection::In, 'out' -> PortDirection::Out, 'bidi' -> PortDirection::Bidi. Tests parse_port_with_direction, parse_port_direction_out, parse_port_direction_bidi pass.

Write failing test: parse_port_with_body - parses `structure S { port x : MechPort { direction = out  param d : Length = 10mm  constraint d > 0mm } }` and asserts: direction from body = Some(PortDirection::Out), members contains 1 Param('d') and 1 Constraint, frame_expr=None.

Implement lower_port_body in ts_parser.rs: iterate named children of port_body node. For param_declaration/let_declaration/constraint_declaration -> delegate to existing lower_param/lower_let/lower_constraint and collect into members vec. For port_direction_setting -> extract 'value' field text and map to PortDirection. For port_frame_setting -> lower 'value' field via lower_expr. Integrate into lower_port: when body is present, call lower_port_body to get (members, body_direction, frame_expr). Body direction overrides inline direction. Test parse_port_with_body passes.

Write failing test: parse_port_with_frame - parses `structure S { port x : MechPort { frame = origin } }` and asserts frame_expr is Some with ExprKind::Ident('origin'). Also test with function call: `frame = frame3(origin)` asserts FunctionCall expression.

Ensure frame expression lowering works in lower_port_body. The port_frame_setting node has a 'value' field containing an expression. Lower it via self.lower_expr(). Store in PortDecl.frame_expr. Test parse_port_with_frame passes.

Write failing test: parse_port_direction_override - parses `structure S { port x : in T { direction = out } }` and asserts direction=Some(PortDirection::Out), confirming body direction overrides inline direction.

Ensure direction override logic is correct in lower_port: body direction takes precedence over inline direction when both are present. The implementation from step-6 should handle this (body_direction.or(inline_direction)). Verify test parse_port_direction_override passes.

Write failing test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/port_compile_tests.rs: compile_port_creates_value_cells - compiles a structure with a port that has body params: `trait MechPort { param diameter : Length } structure def S { port mount : MechPort { param diameter : Length = 5mm } }`. Asserts: TopologyTemplate.ports has 1 CompiledPort with name='mount', direction=Bidi (default), type_name='MechPort'. Port members list has 1 ValueCellDecl with id.member containing 'mount.diameter'.

Add CompiledPort struct to reify-compiler/src/lib.rs (name: String, direction: PortDirection, type_name: String, members: Vec<ValueCellDecl>, frame_expr: Option<CompiledExpr>). Add ports: Vec<CompiledPort> field to TopologyTemplate (initialize empty in compile_structure). Handle MemberDecl::Port in compile_structure's first pass (register port member names in scope as 'port_name.member_name') and second pass (compile port params/lets as ValueCells with composite ValueCellId entity.port_name.member_name, compile constraints, build CompiledPort). Default direction to Bidi if not specified. Push CompiledPort to ports vec. Update TopologyTemplate construction. Update content_hash computation to include port hashes.

Write failing test: compile_port_type_check_known - compiles structure with port referencing a known trait type (defined in same module). No error diagnostics. Also write compile_port_type_check_unknown: port references non-existent trait type -> warning diagnostic about unknown port type.

In port compilation within compile_structure, verify port type_name against the trait_registry HashMap. If type_name not found in trait_registry, emit a warning diagnostic (not error, since trait may be in another module not yet resolved). Test compile_port_type_check_known and compile_port_type_check_unknown pass.

Write failing test: compile_port_member_access - compiles structure with a port and a let binding that references port.member via dot notation: `structure S { port mount : MechPort { param diameter : Length = 5mm }  let d = mount.diameter }`. Asserts the let 'd' compiles without error and its expression is a ValueRef to the correct composite ValueCellId.

Update MemberAccess case in compile_expr_guarded (currently emits error at line 772-781). When object is Ident(name): check if 'name' is a known port name (add port_names: HashSet<String> to CompilationScope, populated during port registration). If so, construct composite key 'name.member' and resolve via scope.resolve(). Return ValueRef with the composite ValueCellId. If name is not a port, fall through to existing error diagnostic. Test compile_port_member_access passes.

Write test: compile_multiple_ports - compiles structure with two ports `port mount : MechPort { param d : Length = 5mm }  port shaft : RotaryPort { param rpm : Length = 100mm }`. Asserts TopologyTemplate.ports has 2 CompiledPorts with distinct names. Port members have unique ValueCellIds (S.mount.d vs S.shaft.rpm).
Verify multiple ports compile correctly. Fix any issues with duplicate name registration, port_names tracking, or ValueCellId conflicts. Test compile_multiple_ports passes.

Write test: compile_port_direction_preserved - compiles structure with `port x : in MechPort { param d : Length = 5mm }`. Asserts CompiledPort.direction == PortDirection::In. Also test out and bidi directions are preserved through compilation.
Ensure port direction from PortDecl is correctly propagated to CompiledPort. Default to Bidi when direction is None. Test compile_port_direction_preserved passes.

design_decisions.decision: Use 'in', 'out', 'bidi' as keywords in the grammar via port_direction_keyword rule
design_decisions.rationale: These become reserved words (can't be used as variable/type names). This is acceptable for a CAD language. The 'in' keyword doesn't conflict with inch unit suffix (80in) because unit suffixes use token.immediate() which is a separate token type. Tree-sitter's context-aware lexer correctly handles keywords vs identifiers based on parser state. Existing keywords like 'param', 'let', 'constraint' already work this way.

design_decisions.decision: Port members use composite ValueCellIds with dotted member names (entity.port_name.member_name)
design_decisions.rationale: Reuses the existing ValueCellId structure (entity + member fields) without requiring structural changes. The member field contains 'port_name.member_name' as a single string, e.g., ValueCellId { entity: 'Motor', member: 'shaft.diameter' }. This enables scope lookup via 'port_name.member_name' keys and preserves the existing scope resolution machinery.

design_decisions.decision: Create named grammar rules for port_direction_keyword, port_direction_setting, port_frame_setting
design_decisions.rationale: Named rules produce named CST nodes that are easy to identify during lowering. Anonymous sequences would require fragile positional child matching. Named rules follow the project's existing pattern (e.g., where_clause, named_argument).

design_decisions.decision: Port type checking emits warning, not error, for unknown type_name
design_decisions.rationale: The port's type_name references a trait that may be defined in another module. Since cross-module resolution isn't implemented yet, we can't definitively say a type is missing. A warning alerts developers while not blocking compilation.

design_decisions.decision: Body direction overrides inline direction when both specified
design_decisions.rationale: If both `port x : in T { direction = out }` are specified, the body direction takes precedence. The body's explicit setting is more intentional and specific.

design_decisions.decision: Port member access limited to same-structure ports (not cross-structure)
design_decisions.rationale: Cross-structure port access (e.g., sub.port.member) requires sub-component type resolution which isn't implemented. This task only enables port member access within the declaring structure via scope-registered dotted names. Cross-structure access is deferred to a future task.

design_decisions.decision: Default direction is Bidi when not specified
design_decisions.rationale: When no direction is given (inline or in body), the port defaults to bidirectional. This is the most permissive default and matches the PortDirection enum semantics from reify-types.

design_decisions.decision: Add port_names HashSet to CompilationScope for member access disambiguation
design_decisions.rationale: The compiler needs to know which identifiers refer to ports (vs regular variables) when compiling MemberAccess expressions. A simple HashSet<String> of port names allows O(1) lookup. When the expression compiler sees `mount.diameter`, it checks if 'mount' is in port_names, and if so, resolves 'mount.diameter' as a composite scope key.

reuse.where: crates/reify-types/src/traits.rs (already defined with In/Out/Bidi variants, re-exported from lib.rs)
reuse.how: Import via reify_types::PortDirection in both parser (for PortDecl.direction) and compiler (for CompiledPort.direction). No changes needed to the enum.

reuse.what: lower_param, lower_let, lower_constraint methods in Lowering impl
reuse.how: Port body members (param_declaration, let_declaration, constraint_declaration) reuse the exact same lowering methods as structure members. Called from lower_port_body.
Port body expressions (param defaults, frame expression, constraint expressions) use the same expression compiler as structure members.

Port members registered in scope with dotted names (port_name.member_name) using the existing register() method with composite string keys. Port member access resolved via the existing scope.resolve() method.
Port members produce standard ValueCellDecl entries with composite ValueCellIds. Port constraints produce standard CompiledConstraint entries. No new types needed for the members themselves.
Same helper function pattern for parsing and compiling test sources. Copy pattern into port_tests.rs and port_compile_tests.rs.

reuse.what: ContentHash, SourceSpan, node_text, span, content_hash utilities in Lowering
reuse.how: Standard CST-to-AST lowering utilities reused for port declaration and body lowering.
Reuse the trait registry already built in the compile() function for port type verification. Pass trait_registry to compile_structure (already passed) and use it to check port type_name.
Follow existing patterns: Diagnostic::error(msg).with_label(DiagnosticLabel::new(span, hint)) or Diagnostic::warning(...). Push to diagnostics vec.
